title: The European Parliament has approved a series of regulations towards the climate goals
bodyHtml:
The European Parliament has approved a series of regulations towards the climate goals.

Among the regulations approved by the Parliament are the Emissions Trading System (ETS), the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), and the Social Climate Fund.

Under the Emissions Trading System (ETS) reform, the free emission allowances given to companies will be gradually phased out from 2026 to 2034. ETS II will also be established in 2027 for road transport and building-related greenhouse gas emissions.

For the aviation sector, the revision of ETS was also accepted. Accordingly, the free emission allowances provided to the aviation sector will be phased out gradually until 2026, and the use of sustainable aviation fuels will be encouraged. In addition, greenhouse gas emissions from the maritime sector will also be included in the ETS.

With the ETS reform, the aim is to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 62 percent by 2030 compared to 2005 levels in the sectors covered.

Another regulation was made in the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM). In this context, the Parliament introduced an obligation for importers of goods and services such as iron, steel, cement, aluminum, fertilizer, electricity, and hydrogen covered by CBAM to pay the price difference that may arise between the carbon price paid in the production country and the carbon price determined within the EU ETS.

This decision is aimed at increasing the climate goals of non-EU countries and reducing imports from countries that do not comply with the EU's low-emission production policies.

Furthermore, the Parliament approved the establishment of a Social Climate Fund (SCF), which will become operational in 2026, aiming to ensure that the climate transition is fair and socially inclusive. Under the decision, the SCF will be financed with revenues of up to 65 billion euros from ETS II. The transferred revenue will provide resources, particularly for low-income households and micro-enterprises affected by energy and transport poverty.

The regulations approved by the Parliament will be submitted to the European Council for approval and will enter into force 20 days after their publication in the Official Journal of the EU.
